followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: First cycle; no prior review to compare against.

main_claims.claim: A post-inflationary quasi–de Sitter phase produces stable massive particles via horizon thermal emission, and these can constitute the observed dark matter abundance.
main_claims.notes: The physical picture is coherent with the Gibbons–Hawking framework, but the actual derivation is not available for inspection. The 'frozen-in' assumption (no number-changing processes) is asserted but its self-consistency with the assumed particle content is not verifiable from the abstract.

main_claims.claim: The horizon temperature in a quasi–de Sitter Universe is inversely proportional to the proper horizon size.
main_claims.notes: For exact de Sitter space the Gibbons–Hawking temperature T = H/(2π) is well established. Its generalization to a quasi-de Sitter phase (w ≠ −1) is a non-trivial extrapolation whose validity requires near-stationarity and is actively debated in the literature (e.g., refs 5, 9–14). Whether the paper rigorously justifies this step is unverifiable without the full text.
main_claims.paper_location: Abstract, assumption (2); implied by section title 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI–DE...'

main_claims.claim: The dark matter mass range spans ~10 keV to near the Planck scale depending on w and T_reh.
main_claims.notes: This wide range is a parameter-space statement, not a single prediction. It is plausible given the latitude in w and T_reh, but the derivation cannot be checked.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The equation-of-state parameter range for the quasi-de Sitter phase is not stated in the abstract (presumably −1 < w < −1/3 for acceleration). The dependence of the result on w should be specified.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract ('equation of state and'—the condition appears to have been truncated in the HTML rendering, likely reading w < −1/3 or similar)
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The allowed range of w determines whether the scenario is physically distinct from pure de Sitter inflation and sets the parameter space for the mass prediction.

missing_assumptions.assumption: No justification is given in the abstract for why the horizon-produced particles decouple immediately (i.e., are 'frozen in') without any thermalization or interaction with the radiation bath.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the produced particles have any coupling to Standard Model radiation (even purely gravitational), they could be thermalized or depleted before radiation domination begins, invalidating the frozen-in number density calculation.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The duration and energy scale of the post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase are implicitly free parameters. The paper does not state what physical mechanism drives this phase or why it must end at the reheat temperature T_reh.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Abstract ('the temperature when radiation domination starts and the quasi–de Sitter phase ends')
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The physical origin and duration of a post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase is model-dependent. Without specifying the model, the result is a parametric mapping rather than a predictive framework.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper rigorously justify the Gibbons–Hawking temperature assignment for a quasi-de Sitter (w ≠ −1) background, including the handling of observer-dependence and the non-stationarity of the background?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The full article text is not available in the bundle; only the abstract and reference list were scraped. The derivation in section 'OBSERVERS IN DE SITTER AND QUASI–DE SITTER...' is inaccessible.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How does the result quantitatively compare to Garny, Sandora, Sloth (2016) [ref 53] and Chung, Kolb, Riotto (1998) [ref 52]? What is the incremental contribution beyond those papers?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The discussion and comparison to prior work is in the article body, which is not available. The abstract does not disambiguate the novel contribution from the existing literature.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is there a typo in reference 2 (Gibbons–Hawking cited as Phys. Rev. D 17 rather than D 15), or does it refer to a different paper?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Reference disambiguation requires access to the published paper's reference list in its final typeset form, not the HTML landing page which may contain rendering artifacts.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What physical mechanism generates the post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase, and is it consistent with standard cosmological constraints (BBN, CMB)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This is discussed in the article body, which is not available.

novelty_assessment: The mechanism—thermal particle production from a cosmological horizon populating a dark matter relic—is not new in concept. Parker (1968/69) established gravitational particle production; Gibbons–Hawking (1977) established the de Sitter horizon temperature; Chung, Kolb, Riotto (1998) and Garny, Sandora, Sloth (2016) applied these ideas to dark matter production during inflation. The specific contribution here is the focus on a post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase with a general equation-of-state parameter w, which shifts the production epoch and allows a different (potentially lower) dark matter mass range than the purely inflationary scenario. If the derivation is correct and the quasi-de Sitter generalization is carefully justified, this represents a meaningful but incremental extension of existing work—not a conceptually new mechanism. The wide mass range (10 keV to M_Pl) limits the sharpness of the prediction. Novelty is moderate.
score_rationale: Score is highly uncertain because the article body was not available—only the abstract and reference list. On the basis of the abstract alone: the physics setup is coherent and within established frameworks (Gibbons–Hawking, gravitational particle production); the mechanism is incremental over prior work; the mass range prediction is very broad and parameter-dependent rather than sharply predictive. The paper is published in Phys. Rev. D and authored by a recognized dark matter theorist, which provides baseline credibility. The moderate technical objections (quasi-de Sitter temperature validity, observer-dependence) are real but may be addressed in the body text. Absent full text, a score of 5/10 (competent but cannot confirm soundness or full novelty) is the most defensible position. Reviewer confidence is low (3/10) due to the missing full text.
summary: Profumo proposes that a stable, massive, non-interacting particle can acquire the observed cosmological dark matter abundance through thermal production by a cosmic quasi–de Sitter horizon during a post-inflationary accelerated-expansion phase. Three explicit assumptions are made: (1) a post-inflationary fluid with equation-of-state parameter w driving accelerated expansion, (2) a horizon temperature inversely proportional to the proper horizon size (Gibbons–Hawking analogue), and (3) static observers. The main quantitative output is a dark matter mass range of ~10 keV to near the Planck scale as a function of w and the reheat temperature. The paper is published in Phys. Rev. D (July 2025). CRITICAL CAVEAT: the bundle contains only the APS abstract landing page; the article body (all equations, derivations, and discussion) is behind the journal paywall and was not scraped. This review is therefore based solely on the abstract, section titles, and reference list. Any judgment about the correctness or completeness of the derivations is speculative.

technical_objections.evidence: The exact Gibbons–Hawking result holds for the maximally symmetric, stationary de Sitter case. Refs 5, 10, 11, 13, 14 in the list deal with subtleties of particle production in de Sitter and quasi-de Sitter backgrounds; it is unclear whether the paper engages adequately with this literature. The derivation is not visible.
technical_objections.objection: Observer-dependence of the Gibbons–Hawking temperature is non-trivial in quasi-de Sitter spacetimes. The 'static observer' idealization (assumption 3) is problematic during a dynamically evolving, non-stationary background. Whether the horizon temperature is well-defined for observers in a quasi-de Sitter phase with w ≠ −1 requires careful justification.

technical_objections.evidence: Inspection of the reference list shows blocks that mirror each other, suggesting either a citation management error or a webpage scraping artifact that duplicated references. The PRD 17 vs PRD 15 discrepancy for Gibbons–Hawking is a concrete inconsistency.
technical_objections.objection: Possible bibliographic error or duplication: Reference 2 cites Gibbons & Hawking as Phys. Rev. D 17, 2738 (1977), while references 27 and 37 cite the same authors with the same page number but volume D 15. The canonical Gibbons–Hawking cosmological horizon paper is PRD 15, 2738 (1977). If Ref. 2 has the wrong volume number, it could represent a mismatch or duplicate. Additionally, refs 7/30 (Medved), 8/28 (Parikh), 9/29 (Sekiwa), and 3/31 (Lapedes) appear to be exact duplicates.

technical_objections.evidence: Garny, Sandora, Sloth (2016) already compute gravitational dark matter production including Gibbons–Hawking contributions during inflation. Chung, Kolb, Riotto (1998) produce superheavy dark matter from the de Sitter phase. The distinction—post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter with general w—may be meaningful, but without the full text the incremental advance cannot be assessed.
technical_objections.objection: The novelty boundary with gravitational particle production from the inflationary phase (Chung, Kolb, Riotto 1998 [ref 52]; Garny, Sandora, Sloth 2016 [ref 53]; Parker 1968/1969 [refs 32/33]) is not clearly demarcated in the abstract. The specific contribution of focusing on a post-inflationary quasi-de Sitter phase with a general w needs to be distinguished from this body of prior work.

technical_objections.evidence: This is standard for gravitational FIMPs, but it means the result is more of a phenomenological mapping than a prediction. The mass range 10 keV to M_Pl is so broad as to be nearly unconstrained.
technical_objections.objection: The 'frozen-in' assumption (no number-changing interactions) is imposed externally and amounts to a free model parameter choice. Without specifying the particle physics model, the mass range prediction is a mapping from (w, T_reh) to mass—not a genuine constraint on dark matter candidates.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract ('does not undergo any number-changing processes')
